events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Delacroix. She is of French-Canadian descent, non-smoker, with a significant family history of breast and ovarian cancer (mother died of ovarian cancer at age 58, maternal aunt had breast cancer at age 44). She works as a high school librarian and lives with her husband. She has two adult children. No prior malignancies. Menopausal status: postmenopausal (natural menopause at age 50).

At age 52 years, the patient had a diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC (AJCC pT3c pN1 M0) HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA OF THE OVARY (ICD-O-3 site code C56.9, histology code 8461/3: Serous cystadenocarcinoma, NOS). Diagnosis confirmed by positive histology from bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal implants. Primary site: bilateral ovaries. Tumor size: right ovary 8.2 cm, left ovary 5.6 cm. Relevant features: ascites present (approximately 2.5 liters on imaging), peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants on the diaphragm, omentum, pelvic peritoneum, and paracolic gutters. Retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (pelvic and para-aortic). Estrogen receptor status: positive. Progesterone receptor: negative. CA-125 at diagnosis: 1,847 U/mL. HE4: 312 pmol/L. ROMA score: elevated (high risk). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY from diagnostic laparoscopy prior to definitive surgery.

At age 52, the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (staging CT), which showed cancer. Findings: bilateral complex ovarian masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm) with solid and cystic components, thick septations, and papillary projections. Large volume ascites. Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking (omental cake measuring 12 x 8 cm), diaphragmatic implants bilaterally (right greater than left, up to 1.8 cm), pelvic peritoneal implants, and implants along the paracolic gutters. Enlarged pelvic lymph nodes (right external iliac 1.4 cm, left internal iliac 1.2 cm). Para-aortic lymph nodes up to 1.1 cm. No evidence of pleural effusion. Liver surface implants noted (largest 0.9 cm). No parenchymal liver or lung metastases. Impression: Findings consistent with advanced ovarian malignancy with peritoneal carcinomatosis, FIGO stage IIIC. No evidence of FIGO stage IV disease.

At age 52, the patient had a pathology result from diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy and omental biopsy. Specimen type: peritoneal implant biopsy (right pelvic peritoneum) and omental core biopsy. Gross description: multiple gray-white firm tissue fragments. Microscopic findings: high-grade serous carcinoma with characteristic slit-like spaces, papillary architecture, psammoma bodies, and marked nuclear atypia with prominent nucleoli. Mitotic rate high (>10 per 10 HPF). Immunohistochemistry: WT1 positive (diffuse, strong), PAX8 positive, p53 aberrant (overexpression pattern, diffuse strong positivity, consistent with TP53 missense mutation), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER positive (30%), PR negative. Ki-67 proliferative index: 75%. Diagnosis: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, consistent with primary ovarian/fallopian tube origin. Note: Morphology and IHC profile support germline BRCA1 testing and molecular profiling.

At age 52 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (germline and somatic) performed for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the peritoneal biopsy (omentum) and peripheral blood (germline). Germline findings: BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756Profs*74), also known as 5382insC, a founder mutation prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish and Eastern European populations - classified as PATHOGENIC per ACMG/AMP criteria. Somatic findings from tumor: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%, pathogenic, consistent with homozygous loss in tumor); BRCA1 somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus confirmed (consistent with biallelic inactivation). Additional somatic alterations: RB1 p.R358* (VAF 22%); CCNE1 amplification (copy number 8.2); MYC gain (copy number 4.1); KRAS wild-type; PIK3CA wild-type; CDK12 wild-type. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 56 (above threshold of 42, HRD-positive). Genomic instability score: high. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 expression: 5% TPS. Actionable alterations: BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (PARP inhibitor sensitive); HRD-positive tumor. No NTRK fusions detected. No ALK or RET rearrangements.

At age 52, the patient had a gynecologic oncology consultation note. History and physical: 52-year-old postmenopausal woman presenting with 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, unintentional weight loss of 12 lbs, and pelvic pressure. Pelvic exam revealed bilateral adnexal masses. Performance status: ECOG 1. BMI: 26.4 kg/m2. No ascites-related respiratory compromise. CA-125 1,847 U/mL. Discussed diagnosis of stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian cancer with patient and husband. Disclosed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC); genetic counseling referral placed. Family history notation: high-penetrance BRCA1 variant with implications for first-degree relatives. Treatment plan discussed: primary debulking surgery (PDS) with cytoreductive intent, targeting R0 resection, followed by platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy. Patient agreeable. Surgical risks discussed including bowel resection, colostomy, ureteral injury. Cancer present. No response data applicable at this initial visit.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent primary debulking surgery (exploratory laparotomy with cytoreductive surgery). Procedure: total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, stripping of diaphragmatic peritoneum bilaterally, resection of pelvic peritoneum, and excision of multiple peritoneal implants from paracolic gutters. Intraoperative findings: 2.2 liters of straw-colored ascites drained. Omental cake completely resected. Right ovarian mass 8 cm, left ovarian mass 6 cm, both arising from fallopian tubes. Multiple diaphragmatic implants stripped. Pelvic sidewall peritoneum resected. Liver surface implant (0.7 cm) excised. No bowel resection required. Estimated blood loss: 650 mL. 1 unit packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 4 hours 42 minutes. Cytoreduction result: R0 (no visible residual disease, complete gross resection). Pathologic confirmation of R0 pending final pathology.

At age 52, the patient had a surgical pathology report from primary debulking surgery specimens. Specimens submitted: right ovary/fallopian tube, left ovary/fallopian tube, uterus/cervix, omentum, appendix, pelvic peritoneum, diaphragmatic peritoneum (right and left), para-aortic lymph nodes (14 nodes), pelvic lymph nodes (22 nodes), liver surface implant. Findings: Right fallopian tube: high-grade serous carcinoma, tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC lesion) identified in the fimbriated end - consistent with fallopian tube origin. Right ovary: high-grade serous carcinoma involving ovarian cortex and surface, 8.1 cm. Left fallopian tube: STIC lesion present. Left ovary: high-grade serous carcinoma, 5.9 cm. Uterus: no carcinoma (leiomyomata incidentally noted). Omentum: high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake, completely excised, 11.5 x 7.8 cm aggregate). Pelvic peritoneum: carcinoma present, margins negative. Diaphragmatic peritoneum: multiple foci of high-grade serous carcinoma, all excised. Liver surface: single focus of serous carcinoma, 0.6 cm, completely excised. Pelvic lymph nodes: 4/22 positive for metastatic carcinoma (largest deposit 1.3 cm). Para-aortic lymph nodes: 2/14 positive. Appendix: no carcinoma. Final pathologic stage: pT3c pN1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition). Cytoreduction: R0 confirmed - no macroscopic residual disease at completion of surgery.

At age 52, the patient had a post-operative visit at 4 weeks after primary debulking surgery. The patient is recovering well. Wound healing intact. No post-operative complications. Bowel function returned on post-operative day 3. Discharged on post-operative day 5. Current performance status ECOG 1. CA-125 post-operatively (day 21): 342 U/mL (significantly reduced from 1,847 U/mL pre-operatively, consistent with R0 resection). Discussed adjuvant chemotherapy plan: carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. Discussed BRCA1-related maintenance options following completion of chemotherapy (PARP inhibitor maintenance). Genetic counseling completed - cascade testing recommended for first-degree relatives. Cancer present (residual microscopic disease expected). Responding to surgical debulking.

At age 52 years, the patient received curative-intent chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days, cycle 1 of 6. Pre-medications: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV. Carboplatin dose calculated by Calvert formula (GFR estimated by CKD-EPI): carboplatin 640 mg IV over 30 minutes; paclitaxel 315 mg IV over 3 hours. Cycle 1 administered without complications. BSA 1.80 m2. Tolerated well. Anti-emetic prophylaxis: ondansetron and dexamethasone.

At age 52, during cycle 2 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (numbness and tingling in fingertips and toes, not interfering with activities of daily living). Also noted: Grade 1 alopecia (total scalp alopecia by cycle 2, consistent with paclitaxel). Grade 1 nausea (well-controlled with antiemetics). No dose reductions required at this time. Neuropathy monitored at each cycle. No neutropenic fever. ANC nadir cycle 2: 1,200 cells/uL (Grade 1 neutropenia). Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, no transfusion required). Platelets: 148,000/uL (within normal limits).

At age 52, after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel (mid-treatment restaging), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer responding to therapy. Findings: No recurrence of ascites. No residual omental mass. No peritoneal implants identified above the limits of CT resolution. No lymphadenopathy in pelvic or para-aortic regions (previously enlarged nodes now within normal limits). Liver: no surface or parenchymal lesions. Diaphragm: no nodular thickening. Post-surgical changes noted in pelvis. Impression: No radiographically evident disease. Response consistent with complete radiographic response to date. CA-125 at time of imaging: 78 U/mL (trending down from 342 U/mL post-surgery). Continued response to platinum-based chemotherapy.

At age 52, mid-treatment clinical note after cycle 3. Patient tolerating chemotherapy reasonably well. Reports persistent Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral feet more than hands), which has been stable and non-progressive. Paclitaxel dose reduction considered but deferred given patient preference to maintain dose density. Fatigue CTCAE Grade 2 (limiting instrumental ADLs). No neutropenic fever episodes. No hospitalizations. CA-125 trending down: 78 U/mL (normal <35 U/mL approaching). Plan: continue carboplatin/paclitaxel cycles 4-6. Reinforce neuropathy monitoring. Discuss PARP inhibitor maintenance planning. Cancer present but responding.

At age 52 years, the patient received cycles 4, 5, and 6 of carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days (completing 6-cycle course). Cycle 4: administered at full dose, Grade 1 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1,050) managed with dose held 1 week then administered. Cycle 5: paclitaxel dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (dose level -1) due to worsening Grade 2-3 peripheral neuropathy (Grade 3 neuropathy with difficulty buttoning clothing and foot drop on left side). Carboplatin maintained at AUC 6. Cycle 6: paclitaxel 135 mg/m2, carboplatin AUC 6, administered per schedule. 6-cycle platinum/taxane chemotherapy course completed. End-of-treatment CA-125: 18 U/mL (within normal range, normalization achieved).

At age 52, after completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel, Grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy documented (difficulty with fine motor tasks, left foot drop requiring physical therapy referral). Neuropathy attributed to cumulative paclitaxel toxicity. Physical therapy initiated. Also noted: complete alopecia (Grade 2, resolving 6-8 weeks post-chemotherapy). Grade 1 fatigue persisting. No long-term renal toxicity from carboplatin (creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, stable). No ototoxicity. Patient referred for neurology consultation for foot drop evaluation - EMG/nerve conduction studies ordered.

At age 52, post-completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel (end-of-treatment restaging CT), the patient had a CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast and PET-CT, which showed no cancer (complete radiographic response). CT findings: No ascites. No peritoneal implants identified. No lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No liver lesions. Post-surgical pelvic changes. PET-CT: No FDG-avid disease identified anywhere. No abnormal metabolic activity in the pelvis, abdomen, chest, or elsewhere. SUVmax of residual post-surgical tissue in pelvis: 1.4 (background). Impression: Complete metabolic and radiographic response to primary debulking surgery and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. No evidence of disease. CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal). Suitable candidate for maintenance PARP inhibitor therapy.

At age 52 (approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis), end-of-chemotherapy assessment. Complete clinical, radiographic, and biochemical response confirmed. CA-125 18 U/mL (normal). PET-CT negative. ECOG performance status 1 (limited by residual neuropathy). Neuropathy improving on physical therapy - foot drop resolving. Discussed maintenance therapy options: given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), HRD-positive tumor, and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, patient is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance. Options reviewed: niraparib (FDA approved for first-line maintenance regardless of BRCA status, with enhanced benefit in BRCA-mutant/HRD-positive), olaparib (FDA approved for BRCA-mutant HRD-positive ovarian cancer, first-line maintenance). Discussed landmark PRIMA and SOLO-1 trial data. Patient elected niraparib based on once-daily dosing convenience. Individualized starting dose discussed - patient weight 72 kg, platelets 189,000/uL - per PRIMA trial protocol individualized starting dose (ISD): niraparib 200 mg once daily (weight <77 kg or platelets <150,000 threshold - patient is borderline, oncologist elected 200 mg ISD to mitigate hematologic toxicity risk). Cancer absent. Complete response.

At age 52 years (approximately 9.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient was initiated on niraparib (PARP inhibitor) maintenance therapy at individualized starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily (standard 300 mg dose reduced per individualized dosing protocol due to weight 72 kg and clinical judgment regarding hematologic risk). Indication: first-line maintenance for FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, following complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy. Labs at start: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Platelets 189,000/uL, ANC 2,400/uL, Creatinine 0.84 mg/dL. Patient counseled on monitoring for hematologic toxicity (thrombocytopenia, anemia, neutropenia), fatigue, nausea, and rare risk of MDS/AML. CBC ordered weekly for first month, then monthly.

At age 52, approximately 3 weeks after initiating niraparib 200 mg daily, the patient developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. CBC results: Platelets 42,000/uL (baseline 189,000/uL at start of niraparib). Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia). ANC 1,800/uL (within normal limits). Patient asymptomatic - no petechiae, bruising, or bleeding. Niraparib held per package insert guidelines (hold for platelets <100,000/uL until recovery to >100,000/uL). Patient instructed to avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, anticoagulants. Repeat CBC in 1 week. No transfusion required. Oncology nurse navigator notified. Patient seen urgently in clinic for CBC review and examination.

At age 52, urgent clinic visit for niraparib-related Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Platelets nadir 42,000/uL at week 3 of niraparib. Physical exam: no petechiae, no ecchymoses, no bleeding. ECOG PS 1. Niraparib held. Bone marrow suppression pattern consistent with niraparib-related hematologic toxicity (expected on-target PARP inhibition effect in bone marrow progenitor cells). Hematology consulted to rule out immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and other etiologies - peripheral smear reviewed: no schistocytes, no platelet clumping, thrombocytopenia consistent with drug-related myelosuppression. Bone marrow biopsy deferred (no evidence of MDS morphology on peripheral smear, no dysplastic features). Weekly CBCs ordered. Plan: resume niraparib at reduced dose (150 mg daily) once platelets recover to >100,000/uL. Reinforce avoidance of platelet-inhibiting medications. Cancer absent.

At age 52, follow-up CBC at 2 weeks after niraparib hold: Platelets recovered to 118,000/uL (recovery from Grade 3 thrombocytopenia nadir of 42,000/uL). Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (stable, Grade 1 anemia). ANC 2,200/uL (normal). Niraparib restarted at dose-reduced level of 150 mg orally once daily per protocol (one dose level reduction from 200 mg starting dose). Patient counseled on continued CBC monitoring. CBC to be checked weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly if stable. No recurrence of severe thrombocytopenia at reduced dose anticipated but close monitoring essential.

At age 52 years (approximately 11 months after initial diagnosis), niraparib resumed at reduced dose of 150 mg orally once daily after platelet recovery. Patient tolerating dose-reduced niraparib. CBC monitoring shows platelet stabilization at 105,000-135,000/uL range (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, <150,000/uL but no further Grade 2 or higher events). Hemoglobin stabilized at 10.5-11.2 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, no transfusion required). Niraparib 150 mg daily continued as long-term maintenance. Planned duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. CA-125 monitoring every 3 months.

At age 53 (approximately 15 months after initial diagnosis, 6 months after starting niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (surveillance imaging), which showed no cancer. Findings: No ascites. No peritoneal implants or carcinomatosis. No pelvic or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No suspicious lesions in liver or elsewhere in abdomen. Post-surgical changes in pelvis unchanged from prior. No new findings. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. CA-125: 14 U/mL (normal, stable). Continued complete remission on niraparib maintenance.

At age 53 (15 months post-diagnosis), routine 3-month surveillance visit on niraparib 150 mg daily. Patient doing well overall. Neuropathy from paclitaxel substantially improved (residual Grade 1 - mild numbness in toes bilaterally, no functional limitation). Foot drop resolved. Hair regrowth complete. Fatigue Grade 1 (minimal, not limiting activities). Tolerating niraparib 150 mg well at this dose level. Platelets stable at 112,000/uL (Grade 1, asymptomatic). CBC trend stable - no further dose reductions required. CA-125: 14 U/mL (normal). CT negative for disease. ECOG PS 0-1. Menopausal symptoms: hot flashes and vaginal dryness managed with non-hormonal agents (venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms; vaginal lubricants). Discussed BRCA1 implications - patient's daughter (age 24) has undergone genetic counseling and BRCA1 testing - test result positive (confirmed 5382insC variant). Referral to high-risk program for daughter placed. Cancer absent. In complete remission.

At age 53 (21 months after initial diagnosis, 12 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (annual surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: Stable post-operative changes in pelvis. No ascites. No peritoneal nodules or masses. No lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, kidneys normal. No adnexal masses (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy). Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. CA-125: 12 U/mL (normal, trending stable/slightly down). Patient remains in complete radiographic and biochemical remission on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 53 (21 months post-diagnosis, 12 months of niraparib maintenance), annual surveillance visit. Patient in complete remission. CA-125 12 U/mL. CT negative. Niraparib 150 mg daily well-tolerated at this dose. Thrombocytopenia stable at Grade 1 (platelets 108,000-125,000/uL, no new Grade 2+ events since dose reduction). Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (stable Grade 1 anemia, erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not initiated given stability). No cytopenias requiring further dose reduction. Quality of life discussion: patient reports good functional status (returned to full-time work as librarian), ECOG 0. Peripheral neuropathy Grade 1 residual, not progressing. Cognitive function intact. Psychosocial: mild anxiety related to cancer recurrence fear, referred to oncology social work/support group for ovarian cancer survivors. BRCA1-related counseling: discussed risk-reducing mastectomy option per NCCN guidelines for BRCA1 carriers; patient is post-bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (already accomplished as part of cytoreductive surgery); breast surveillance with annual mammography and MRI recommended; patient up-to-date with breast MRI (negative). Cancer absent. Complete remission. Continue niraparib maintenance.

At age 54 (approximately 27 months post-diagnosis, 18 months of niraparib maintenance), routine surveillance visit. Patient continues in complete remission. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal, stable). No new symptoms. Niraparib 150 mg daily continued without further modification. CBC: Platelets 119,000/uL (stable Grade 1), Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL (stable Grade 1 anemia), ANC 2,100/uL (normal). No evidence of MDS on routine CBC differential - no dysplastic changes. Quality of life: excellent, ECOG 0. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 residual, essentially resolved (patient reports only very mild tingling in toes with prolonged standing, not bothersome). Patient enrolled in BRCA1 ovarian cancer survivor registry study. Breast surveillance: breast MRI performed 2 months prior, negative. Discussed option of prophylactic mastectomy again - patient deferred, prefers surveillance approach. Next surveillance imaging in 6 months. Cancer absent. Durable complete remission on PARP inhibitor maintenance.

documents.text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
MID-TREATMENT RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC). Status post primary debulking surgery with R0 resection followed by adjuvant Paraplatin/PTX chemotherapy, now completing cycle 3 of 6. Mid-treatment restaging examination. Pre-treatment CA-125 1,847 U/mL; post-surgical CA-125 342 U/mL; current CA-125 78 U/mL.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350). Imaging obtained in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformations generated and reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Oral contrast not administered. Comparison made to prior CT abdomen/pelvis (staging examination) and post-operative imaging.

FINDINGS:

PELVIS:
The patient has undergone total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with bilateral pelvic peritoneal resection. Expected post-operative anatomic changes are present with surgical clips in the expected region of the bilateral adnexa and along the pelvic sidewalls. No discrete pelvic mass or soft tissue nodularity is identified. The bladder is well-distended and unremarkable. No free pelvic fluid. Previously noted enlarged right external iliac lymph node (1.4 cm) and left internal iliac lymph node (1.2 cm) are no longer enlarged -- nodes are now at most 0.6-0.7 cm in short axis and appear morphologically normal in configuration. No new pelvic lymphadenopathy.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No ascites. The large-volume ascites noted on the staging examination has completely resolved. No peritoneal nodularity or soft tissue thickening is appreciated along the parietal or visceral peritoneal surfaces, including the paracolic gutters bilaterally, the pelvic cul-de-sac, and the small bowel mesentery. No omental nodularity or mass. The omental region has been surgically resected (infracolic omentectomy); the residual omental fat is unremarkable with no nodular soft tissue deposits to suggest residual or recurrent peritoneal implants.

DIAPHRAGM:
No diaphragmatic nodularity or thickening. The previously noted diaphragmatic peritoneal implants (right greater than left, up to 1.8 cm on the staging scan) are not visualized on the current examination. Post-stripping changes are noted. No subphrenic fluid collections.

LIVER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
The liver is normal in size and contour. No focal parenchymal lesions. The liver surface is smooth; no surface nodularity to suggest implants. The previously excised liver surface implant (0.7 cm at surgery) site shows a tiny area of post-operative change without discrete nodularity. Intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. Common bile duct measures 4 mm (normal).

SPLEEN:
Unremarkable. No focal lesions. Normal size.

PANCREAS:
Visualized portions of the pancreas are unremarkable. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal nodules or masses.

KIDNEYS:
Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast normally. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No perinephric abnormality. No ureteral obstruction.

RETROPERITONEUM AND LYMPH NODES:
Previously enlarged para-aortic lymph nodes (up to 1.1 cm on staging CT) are not visualized as discrete enlarged nodes on the current study. A few small sub-centimeter retroperitoneal nodes are identified, none exceeding 7 mm in short axis, and these are within normal size limits. No coalescent nodal masses. Aortocaval interval is clear.

BOWEL:
Small and large bowel are unremarkable. No obstruction. No wall thickening. No mesenteric infiltration. Appendix has been surgically removed (appendectomy). No post-operative complications evident.

ABDOMINAL WALL AND SOFT TISSUES:
The midline surgical incision site demonstrates expected post-operative changes with skin staple artifact and subcutaneous tissue changes. No wound dehiscence, hematoma, or fluid collection at the incision site. No herniation through the fascial defect.

CHEST (LUNG BASES, INCLUDED ON THIS EXAMINATION):
Imaged lung bases are clear. No pleural effusion bilaterally. No consolidation at the lung bases. No pulmonary nodules are seen in the visualized portions of the lower lobes.

BONE:
Visualized osseous structures are without aggressive-appearing lytic or blastic lesions. No suspicious bony abnormality.

IMPRESSION:

1. Markedly improved appearance compared to prior staging examination. No radiographically evident peritoneal carcinomatosis. The previously documented omental caking, extensive peritoneal implants (diaphragmatic, paracolic, pelvic), ascites, and pelvic lymphadenopathy have all resolved -- appearance consistent with excellent response to primary debulking surgery and ongoing platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy.

2. Pelvic and para-aortic lymph nodes previously enlarged are now within normal size limits.

3. No ascites.

4. No new hepatic surface lesions; no parenchymal metastases identified.

5. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis and abdomen are as expected and without evidence of post-operative complication.

6. CA-125 trending down significantly (1,847 --> 342 --> 78 U/mL), consistent with ongoing biochemical response.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a very favorable treatment response following cytoreductive surgery and three cycles of systemic chemotherapy. No radiographically evident residual or recurrent disease identified at this time.

Dictated and electronically signed by:
Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
Department of Radiology

GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY - CHEMOTHERAPY VISIT NOTE
Mid-Treatment Assessment: Paraplatin/Taxol Cycle 3 of 6

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Margaret Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1-positive, 5382insC), status post R0 primary debulking surgery followed by adjuvant Paraplatin/pacli chemotherapy. She presents today for mid-treatment assessment following completion of her third cycle of carbo/PTX.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Delacroix was diagnosed with advanced high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma approximately 5 months ago, with initial CA-125 of 1,847 U/mL and large-volume ascites on staging CT. She underwent uncomplicated primary debulking surgery approximately 4.5 months ago achieving complete gross resection (R0), with final surgical pathology confirming pT3c pN1 M0 disease (6 of 36 lymph nodes positive; omental cake resected en bloc; diaphragmatic peritoneal stripping performed). Post-operative CA-125 on day 21 declined to 342 U/mL.

She began adjuvant carbo AUC 6 / pacli 175 mg/m2 IV q21 days approximately 10 weeks ago. Cycle 1 was administered without significant incident. During cycle 2, she developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy with bilateral hand and foot paresthesias (numbness and tingling at fingertips and toes, not interfering with ADLs), as well as complete scalp alopecia consistent with pacli and Grade 1 nausea adequately controlled with scheduled antiemetics. Dose reductions were deferred at the time of cycle 2 given the neuropathy was non-functional and the patient wished to continue full-dose therapy. She received cycle 3 at full dose (Paraplatin 640 mg, Taxol 315 mg based on BSA 1.80 m2) approximately 3 weeks ago and has now returned for mid-treatment assessment.

Since cycle 3, she reports the following:
- Peripheral neuropathy: persistent and somewhat bothersome, bilateral feet greater than hands. She describes a constant baseline numbness in both soles and all toes, with intermittent "electric-like" tingling. She reports mild difficulty with balance on uneven surfaces. She is not experiencing frank weakness, foot drop, or difficulty walking on flat ground. No impairment of fine motor tasks such as buttoning at this time.
- Fatigue: she rates fatigue as moderately limiting - she has been able to perform her light household tasks but has taken a leave of absence from work as a librarian since starting chemotherapy. She naps daily. This is consistent with CTCAE Grade 2 fatigue.
- Nausea: minimal, Grade 1, well-controlled with ondansetron PRN and scheduled dexamethasone. No vomiting. Appetite mildly decreased but maintaining weight.
- Alopecia: complete scalp hair loss. Eyebrow and eyelash thinning. She is using a wig and is adjusting emotionally.
- Bowel habits: some constipation requiring fiber supplementation and MiraLAX. No diarrhea. No blood in stool.
- Musculoskeletal: diffuse mild myalgia post-infusion, resolving by day 5 each cycle.
- Infectious symptoms: no fever, no chills, no cough, no dysuria, no neutropenic symptoms reported.
- No hospitalizations since starting chemotherapy.

She also asks about next steps after completing chemotherapy, including maintenance therapy. We have previously discussed nira as a maintenance option given her germline BRCA1 status and the expectation of completing chemotherapy with response. She has reviewed written materials provided at the last visit.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: fatigue (Grade 2), peripheral neuropathy bilateral hands/feet (Grade 2, feet > hands), alopecia (complete), mild nausea (Grade 1), constipation, decreased appetite, myalgia post-infusion (resolving).
Negative: no fever, no night sweats, no dyspnea, no chest pain, no palpitations, no visual changes, no headache, no dizziness, no syncope, no edema, no abdominal pain, no new pelvic pain, no hematuria, no melena, no hematochezia, no oral mucositis, no rash, no jaundice. No symptoms of bowel obstruction.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing, pleasant woman in no acute distress. Wearing a wig. Mildly fatigued appearance. Alert and oriented.
Vitals: Temperature 36.9°C, BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 71.8 kg (stable; baseline 72 kg at start of chemotherapy). BMI 25.9 kg/m2.
HEENT: Alopecia, minimal residual eyebrow hair. Oropharynx without mucositis or thrush. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, no wheezing.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A - Right Pelvic Peritoneal Implant:
Received in formalin, labeled "right pelvic peritoneum biopsy," are multiple irregular gray-white to tan firm tissue fragments, in aggregate measuring 1.4 x 0.9 x 0.3 cm. Cut surfaces are homogeneously tan-white with a firm, fibrous consistency. No cystic areas, no necrosis, and no grossly discernible hemorrhage. Entirely submitted in one cassette.

Specimen B - Omental Core Biopsy:
Received in formalin, labeled "omental biopsy," are two core needle biopsy fragments, each measuring approximately 1.2 cm in length and 0.2 cm in diameter, tan-white, firm to rubbery in texture, with a heterogeneous cut surface demonstrating areas of gray-white firm tissue alternating with pale yellow adipose tissue. Both cores entirely submitted in one cassette.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A - Right Pelvic Peritoneal Implant:
Sections show fibrous peritoneal tissue extensively infiltrated by carcinoma arranged in complex papillary and micropapillary fronds, solid nests, and slit-like glandular spaces. The papillary structures are lined by markedly atypical epithelial cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, markedly irregular nuclear contours, coarse chromatin, and conspicuous eosinophilic macronucleoli. Cytoplasm is scant to moderate and eosinophilic. Mitotic figures are numerous and readily identifiable, including atypical forms; mitotic count exceeds 10 per 10 high-power fields. Several psammoma bodies (concentrically laminated calcifications) are identified within the papillary cores. A desmoplastic stromal reaction is present at the peritoneal interface. There is no evidence of mucinous differentiation, clear cell morphology, or endometrioid features. No lymphovascular invasion is identified in this limited specimen.

Specimen B - Omental Core Biopsy:
Sections of omental adipose tissue show near-complete replacement by carcinoma with identical morphology to Specimen A. The tumor demonstrates papillary and solid architecture with slit-like spaces characteristic of high-grade serous carcinoma. Nuclear atypia is severe with marked pleomorphism, irregular chromatin distribution, and large prominent nucleoli. Mitotic rate is high (>10/10 HPF). Occasional psammoma bodies are noted. Extensive desmoplastic fibrosis surrounds nests and sheets of tumor cells. Background omental fat is present at the periphery of the cores, confirming omental origin. No necrosis identified in these small biopsy specimens. No mucinous or clear cell features.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES - IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY:

Immunohistochemistry was performed on Specimen A (right pelvic peritoneal implant) using validated antibody clones on formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue sections with appropriate positive and negative controls.

  WT1 (clone WT49):                POSITIVE - diffuse, strong nuclear staining (3+) in >90% of tumor cells
  PAX8 (clone MRQ-50):             POSITIVE - diffuse, strong nuclear staining (3+)
  CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30):        POSITIVE - diffuse, strong cytoplasmic staining
  CK20 (clone Ks20.8):             NEGATIVE - no staining in tumor cells (positive internal control: colonic mucosa)
  p53 (clone DO-7):                ABERRANT OVEREXPRESSION PATTERN - diffuse, strong nuclear staining in >80% of tumor cells (consistent with TP53 missense "gain-of-function" mutation; this staining pattern is distinct from the null/complete absence pattern seen with truncating mutations)
  ER (estrogen receptor, clone SP1): POSITIVE - nuclear staining in approximately 30% of tumor cells (H-score approximately 40; low-level expression)
  PR (progesterone receptor, clone 16):  NEGATIVE - no nuclear staining
  Ki-67 (clone MIB-1):             POSITIVE - proliferative index approximately 75% of tumor nuclei
  BerEP4 (EpCAM):                  POSITIVE - diffuse membranous staining
  Calretinin:                      NEGATIVE (negative in tumor cells; supports carcinoma over mesothelioma)
  D2-40 (podoplanin):              NEGATIVE in tumor cells
  Napsin A:                        NEGATIVE
  TTF-1:                           NEGATIVE
  CDX2:                            NEGATIVE

INTERPRETATION OF IHC: The immunoprofile (WT1+, PAX8+, CK7+, CK20-, p53 aberrant overexpression, ER low-positive, PR negative, calretinin negative, napsin A negative, TTF-1 negative, CDX2 negative) is consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin (ovarian/fallopian tube/primary peritoneal). The aberrant p53 overexpression pattern is a hallmark of TP53 mutation, which is present in >96% of high-grade serous carcinomas. This profile effectively excludes primary mucinous carcinoma, mesothelioma, metastatic colorectal carcinoma, lung adenocarcinoma, and other common peritoneal tumor types.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS:

A. RIGHT PELVIC PERITONEAL IMPLANT BIOPSY:
   - HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA
   - Morphology and immunophenotype consistent with primary ovarian/fallopian tube/peritoneal origin (Mullerian)
   - See ancillary studies and note below

B. OMENTAL CORE BIOPSY:
   - HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA
   - Morphology identical to Specimen A
   - Extensive replacement of omental adipose tissue by carcinoma

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSTIC NOTE:

The histologic features and immunohistochemical profile of both specimens are diagnostic of high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC). The combination of complex papillary architecture, slit-like spaces, prominent psammoma bodies, severe nuclear atypia (FIGO nuclear grade 3), high mitotic index (>10/10 HPF), and the characteristic p53 aberrant overexpression pattern fulfills established morphologic criteria for HGSC per the WHO Classification of Tumours of the Female Genital Tract.

In the setting of bilateral complex adnexal masses with peritoneal carcinomatosis and the clinical/radiographic picture provided, the findings are most consistent with primary ovarian or fallopian tube high-grade serous carcinoma with peritoneal dissemination. Definitive assignment of primary site (ovary vs. fallopian tube vs. primary peritoneal) will require examination of the primary surgical specimen (ovaries, fallopian tubes) at the time of definitive cytoreductive surgery.

The low-level estrogen receptor positivity (approximately 30%, H-score ~40) is within the range commonly observed in HGSC and does not alter the diagnosis or histologic classification.

Molecular testing (germline BRCA1/BRCA2 and somatic tumor profiling) is clinically indicated for this diagnosis per current guidelines.

METHODOLOGY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Platform: Hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing panel covering 523 cancer-
relevant genes (coding exons and selected intronic/splice regions).
Sequencing performed on Illumina NovaSeq 6000.
Mean sequencing depth: 600x (tumor); 150x (germline).
Minimum reportable VAF: 5% (somatic); 20% (germline).
Additional analyses: copy number variation (CNV) by read-depth normalization;
structural variant (SV) detection by split-read and discordant-pair analysis;
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) scoring using genome-wide SNP-based
loss-of-heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale
state transitions (LST); tumor mutational burden (TMB); microsatellite instability
(MSI) by fragment-size analysis of 114 microsatellite loci; PD-L1 IHC performed
concurrently (22C3 pharmDx assay, reported as tumor proportion score [TPS]).

Germline variant classification follows ACMG/AMP 2015 guidelines (Richards et al.).
Somatic variant interpretation follows OncoKB and ClinVar criteria. Actionability
tiers follow the AMP/ASCO/CAP 2017 consensus framework.

*** PATHOGENIC GERMLINE VARIANT IDENTIFIED ***

Gene: BRCA1
Transcript: NM_007294.4
Variant: c.5266dupC  (p.Gln1756Profs*74)
Common Alias: 5382insC; rs80357906
Zygosity: HETEROZYGOUS (germline)
VAF (blood): 48.3%  (consistent with constitutional heterozygosity)
Classification: PATHOGENIC — Class 5 (ACMG/AMP criteria: PVS1, PS1, PM2, PP5)

Interpretation:
The c.5266dupC (5382insC) variant introduces a single-cytosine insertion in exon 20
of BRCA1, resulting in a frameshift at codon 1756 and a premature termination codon
74 amino acids downstream (p.Gln1756Profs*74). This is expected to produce a
non-functional truncated BRCA1 protein subject to nonsense-mediated mRNA decay.
This variant is one of the most prevalent BRCA1 founder mutations worldwide,
especially in populations of Ashkenazi Jewish, Eastern European (Polish, Russian),
and French-Canadian descent. It is extensively documented in the literature as
conferring a markedly elevated lifetime risk of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma
(~44% by age 70) and breast carcinoma (~65–72% by age 70). Functional studies
consistently demonstrate abrogation of homologous recombination DNA repair.

Germline Genetic Counseling Note: This finding has implications for the patient's
first-degree biological relatives. Cascade genetic testing is recommended. The
patient should be referred to a certified genetic counselor for result disclosure
and family communication assistance. (Disclosure and counseling referral are the
responsibility of the ordering provider.)

No additional pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants identified in
other hereditary cancer genes (including BRCA2, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, CHEK2,
ATM, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM) on this panel.

DETAILED SOMATIC VARIANT INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TP53 p.Arg248Trp (VAF 68.2%) — Tier I
The p.R248W missense substitution in TP53 is among the most frequently documented
gain-of-function hotspot mutations in human cancer, and is the predominant TP53
alteraton in high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin. VAF of 68.2% in a
tumor with estimated 65% purity indicates near-homozygous allelic representation,
consistent with loss of the remaining wild-type allele (LOH at 17p13.1). This is
consistent with the aberrant diffuse-strong p53 overexpression pattern observed on
immunohistochemistry (reported separately in pathology report SP-PATH-001).
Absolute requirement for TP53 mutation in high-grade serous carcinoma is well-
established; this finding confirms the diagnosis. Not a PARP inhibitor sensitizer
but confirms HGSC lineage.

BRCA1 Somatic LOH (17q12) — Tier I (Biallelic BRCA1 Inactivation)
Copy number analysis demonstrates allele-specific loss of heterozygosity
encompassing the BRCA1 locus on chromosome 17q12-21. In the context of the
confirmed germline c.5266dupC pathogenic variant (heterozygous), somatic loss of
the wild-type BRCA1 allele constitutes complete biallelic inactivation of BRCA1.
This is the canonical "two-hit" mechanism of tumor suppressor gene inactivation
(Knudson model). Biallelic BRCA1 loss abrogates homologous recombination DNA double-
strand break repair, resulting in dependence on alternative (error-prone) repair
pathways including PARP1-mediated base excision repair. This provides the mechanistic
basis for synthetic lethality with PARP inhibitors. This finding, combined with the
HRD-positive genomic scar signature (HRD score 56), strongly supports PARP inhibitor
therapeutic benefit.

RB1 p.Arg358* (VAF 21.7%) — Tier II
Nonsense mutation resulting in premature truncation of the retinoblastoma protein.
RB1 loss is a recurrent secondary event in HGSC and is associated with cell cycle
deregulation and G1/S checkpoint abrogation. Subclonal VAF (21.7%) suggests this
may represent a clonal subpopulation. Not directly actionable with currently approved
agents but is noted for completeness and potential prognostic relevance. RB1 loss
has been associated with relative resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors (not applicable
here) and may influence platinum sensitivity in some studies.

CCNE1 Amplification (Copy Number 8.2) — Tier II
High-level focal amplification of CCNE1 (cyclin E1) on chromosome 19q12. CCNE1
amplification is present in approximately 20% of HGSC and is associated with
replication stress and genomic instability. CCNE1 amplification has been associated
with relative platinum resistance in some series; however, the prognostic impact in
the setting of HRD-positive, BRCA1-mutant HGSC remains to be definitively
established. No FDA-approved targeted agent for CCNE1 amplification currently.
WEE1 inhibitors (e.g., adavosertib) and CDK2 inhibitors are under investigation for
CCNE1-amplified HGSC in clinical trials. No CCNE1-targeted therapy approved at this
time.

MYC Gain (Copy Number 4.1) — Tier III (VUS/Copy Gain)
Moderate gain at 8q24.21 (MYC locus), consistent with low-level copy gain rather
than high-level focal amplification. MYC gain is extremely common in HGSC as part
of widespread chromosomal instability. Not independently actionable. Included for
completeness.

HOMOLOGOUS RECOMBINATION DEFICIENCY (HRD) ANALYSIS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HRD Score (Myriad myChoice-equivalent composite): 56
Threshold for HRD-positive designation: ≥42
Result: HRD-POSITIVE

The HRD score of 56 exceeds the validated threshold of 42 for HRD-positive
designation. The composite score reflects the cumulative "genomic scar" imparted
by long-standing BRCA1 dysfunction: elevated LOH (31), elevated telomeric allelic
imbalance (TAI = 14), and elevated large-scale state transitions (LST = 11).
HRD-positive status in conjunction with germline/somatic BRCA1 biallelic
inactivation is the strongest available predictor of benefit from PARP inhibitor
therapy in HGSC, as established in the PRIMA, SOLO-1, and PAOLA-1 clinical trials.

   context of somatic loss of the wild-type allele (LOH), results in complete

   inhibitor strategies.

5. MSS / Low TMB / Low PD-L1: Profile does not support current checkpoint

This genomic profile is highly consistent with BRCA1-driven, HRD-positive, platinum-
and PARP inhibitor-sensitive high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. Germline findings
have important hereditary implications for biological relatives.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman of French-Canadian descent referred to Gynecologic Oncology for evaluation and management of newly diagnosed high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC. She presents today accompanied by her husband.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman (natural menopause at age 50) who presented approximately 3 months ago with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 pounds, and a sense of pelvic pressure and heaviness. She initially attributed her symptoms to menopausal changes and dietary indiscretion. Her primary gynecologist noted bilateral adnexal masses on pelvic examination and referred her for further workup.

Serum tumor markers at that time were markedly elevated: CA-125 1,847 U/mL and HE4 312 pmol/L, with a ROMA score in the high-risk range. Pelvic ultrasound and subsequent CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast confirmed bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm) with thick septations, solid components, and papillary projections. Imaging further demonstrated large-volume ascites, omental caking (approximately 12 x 8 cm), extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants on the diaphragm bilaterally (right-sided dominant, up to 1.8 cm), pelvic peritoneum, paracolic gutters, and liver surface (largest implant 0.9 cm). Pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy was identified (right external iliac node 1.4 cm, left internal iliac 1.2 cm, para-aortic up to 1.1 cm). No evidence of distant hematogenous metastases; no pleural effusions.

Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal and omental biopsy was performed. Pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with characteristic papillary architecture, slit-like glandular spaces, psammoma bodies, and marked nuclear atypia. Immunoprofile: WT1 positive (diffuse strong), PAX8 positive, p53 aberrant (overexpression pattern, consistent with TP53 missense mutation), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER positive (30%), PR negative. Ki-67 75%. Diagnosis: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, consistent with primary ovarian/fallopian tube origin, FIGO stage IIIC.

Germline and somatic NGS was subsequently sent given the morphologic and IHC profile. Germline testing revealed a BRCA1 pathogenic variant: c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756Profs*74), the so-called 5382insC Eastern European/French-Canadian founder mutation, classified as pathogenic per ACMG/AMP criteria. Somatic tumor profiling confirmed TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%), BRCA1 somatic LOH, RB1 truncating mutation (p.R358*, VAF 22%), and CCNE1 amplification (copy number ~8). HRD score 56 (above the 42 threshold, HRD-positive). TMB 4.2 mut/Mb (low). MSS. PD-L1 TPS 5%.

Patient was referred to us for surgical and medical oncology management.

She has no prior history of malignancy. She denies prior use of oral contraceptives for a prolonged duration. She has not undergone prior pelvic or abdominal surgery. No prior chemotherapy or radiation.

At today's visit she reports the abdominal distension remains significant and uncomfortable. She has mild dyspnea on exertion with exertion (climbing more than one flight of stairs), which she attributes to elevated diaphragm from ascites. No pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or frank orthopnea. She continues to lose weight slowly. Appetite is poor. Bowel and bladder function intact, though she notes urinary frequency. No significant vaginal bleeding.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with newly confirmed FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary/fallopian tube, with germline BRCA1 5382insC pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor. Her presentation is classic: bilateral adnexal masses, massive ascites, omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis, retroperitoneal nodal involvement, and a markedly elevated CA-125.

I have had an extensive discussion with Ms. Delacroix and her husband today. We reviewed the diagnosis, the implications of the germline BRCA1 finding, the treatment strategy, and the goals of therapy. The patient is understandably distressed but engaged and asking thoughtful questions.

1. DIAGNOSIS: FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma, ovary/fallopian tube origin
   - Confirmed by histology (biopsy from diagnostic laparoscopy): WT1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant, CK7+, ER 30%+
   - Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC) confirmed - significant implications for treatment selection (platinum/PARP sensitivity), surveillance, and family cascade testing
   - HRD-positive tumor (score 56) further supports PARP inhibitor sensitivity

2. SURGICAL PLAN - PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
   - Given patient's ECOG 1 performance status, absence of significant comorbidities, and imaging assessment suggesting potentially resectable disease, patient is an appropriate candidate for upfront primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS).
   - Goal: R0 resection (no visible residual disease), which is strongly associated with improved overall survival in advanced HGSOC.
   - Planned procedure: Exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, stripping of diaphragmatic peritoneum, resection of pelvic peritoneal implants, appendectomy, and excision of any additional implants as dictated by intraoperative findings.
   - Surgical risks discussed at length: general anesthetic risks, bleeding, infection, wound dehiscence, venous thromboembolism, ureteral injury, bowel injury (possible bowel resection with or without ostomy depending on intraoperative findings), bladder injury, conversion to open procedure if not already planned open approach, incomplete cytoreduction if disease is more extensive than anticipated intraoperatively. Patient verbalized understanding.
   - Patient consented to proceed with PDS.
   - Pre-operative bowel preparation and VTE prophylaxis will be arranged.
   - Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation requested.
   - Nutrition consult requested given albumin 3.4 and recent weight loss; will attempt nutritional optimization prior to surgery.

3. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY (POST-OPERATIVE PLAN):
   - Following surgery, standard of care adjuvant chemotherapy: Carboplatin AUC 6 / Paclitaxel (Taxol) 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days x 6 cycles
   - Chemotherapy to begin approximately 4-6 weeks post-operatively once patient has adequately recovered
   - Anti-emetic prophylaxis, growth factor support per institutional protocol as needed
   - Discussed common side effects: hematologic toxicity (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia), peripheral neuropathy (cumulative paclitaxel toxicity - a concern to monitor closely), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, nephrotoxicity (carboplatin-related), hypersensitivity reactions

4. MAINTENANCE THERAPY (PARP INHIBITOR - DEFERRED DECISION POST-CHEMO):
   - Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive status, patient is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy, contingent on achieving at least a partial response.
   - Options to be discussed at completion of chemotherapy: niraparib (Zejula) or olaparib (Lynparza) - both FDA-approved in the first-line maintenance setting for BRCA-mutant/HRD-positive advanced ovarian cancer.
   - Will defer definitive maintenance therapy discussion until end of chemotherapy, once response is confirmed.

5. GERMLINE BRCA1 - GENETIC COUNSELING AND FAMILY CASCADE TESTING:
   - Referral placed to Certified Genetic Counselor for formal genetic counseling visit.
   - Patient counseled today on the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern of BRCA1 pathogenic variants: ~50% chance of transmission to each first-degree relative.
   - Her adult children (daughter age 24, son age 21) should be offered cascade testing.
   - Patient's siblings, if any, should also be offered testing.
   - Breast surveillance for BRCA1 carriers: annual mammography + breast MRI per NCCN guidelines (risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy already effectively accomplished as part of PDS).

6. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - Social work referral placed (psychosocial support, coping resources, cancer support groups).
   - Nutrition consult requested.
   - Physical therapy referral to be placed post-operatively for prehabilitation/rehabilitation.
   - Advance care planning discussion initiated - patient will complete health care proxy documents prior to surgical admission.
   - Ibuprofen discontinued given planned surgery and risk of perioperative bleeding.

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Surgical date to be coordinated with OR scheduling within the next 1-2 weeks.
   - Patient to return for pre-operative visit.
   - Repeat CA-125 at time of post-operative visit to establish post-surgical baseline.

Time spent with patient and family in this consultation: 75 minutes (including review of records, examination, counseling, and care coordination).

All questions answered. Patient and husband express understanding of diagnosis and plan and wish to proceed.

GROSS DESCRIPTION

Part A — Right fallopian tube and right ovary: Received fresh in a labeled container. The right fallopian tube measures 7.5 cm in length and 0.8 cm in diameter. The fimbriated end is distorted by a 0.6 cm papillary excrescence with firm, granular texture. The right ovary is enlarged, measuring 8.1 x 6.2 x 4.9 cm, and is replaced by a multiloculated cystic and solid mass. The external surface shows multiple gray-white papillary excrescences and areas of surface involvement. On sectioning, the mass contains thick, hemorrhagic fluid, solid tan-white areas with geographic necrosis, and calcified foci (grossly suggestive of psammoma bodies). Representative sections in 12 cassettes.

Part B — Left fallopian tube and left ovary: The left fallopian tube measures 6.8 cm in length. The fimbriated end contains a 0.4 cm area of mucosal roughening. The left ovary is enlarged, measuring 5.9 x 4.1 x 3.6 cm, with a bosselated external surface and firm, gray-white solid areas. On sectioning, solid and cystic areas noted with focal necrosis. Representative sections in 8 cassettes.

Part C — Uterus and cervix: The uterus with attached cervix and bilateral parametria weighs 98 grams and measures 8.5 x 5.2 x 3.8 cm. The serosal surface is smooth and glistening with no tumor deposits identified grossly. The endometrium is atrophic, measuring 0.2 cm in maximum thickness. The myometrium is heterogeneous with multiple well-defined tan-white intramural nodules ranging from 0.4 to 1.8 cm (consistent with leiomyomata). No endometrial or myometrial lesions grossly suspicious for carcinoma. The cervix is smooth and unremarkable. Representative sections in 8 cassettes.

Part D — Infracolic omentum: Received as a large, fibrofatty specimen measuring 32 x 18 x 3.5 cm in aggregate. The cut surface shows diffuse replacement of omental fat by firm, tan-white to gray, infiltrative tumor tissue with a glistening cut surface in areas. Aggregate tumor mass measures approximately 11.5 x 7.8 x 2.9 cm. Margins are inked. Sections in 10 cassettes including margins.

Part E — Appendix: The vermiform appendix measures 5.8 cm in length and 0.7 cm in diameter. The serosal surface is smooth and tan. On cross-section, the lumen is patent and the wall is unremarkable. No mucocele or intraluminal mass. Representative sections in 2 cassettes.

Part F — Right pelvic peritoneum: Multiple irregular gray-white fragments of fibrofatty peritoneum, in aggregate measuring 9.5 x 7.0 x 1.0 cm. Focal areas of nodular thickening are present. Representative sections in 4 cassettes.

Part G — Left pelvic peritoneum: Multiple peritoneal fragments in aggregate measuring 7.8 x 5.5 x 0.8 cm with focal nodularity. Representative sections in 3 cassettes.

Part H — Right diaphragmatic peritoneum: Multiple thin peritoneal strips, in aggregate 14 x 8 cm, with scattered gray-white nodular deposits ranging from 0.2 to 0.9 cm. All deposits submitted en face in 4 cassettes.

Part I — Left diaphragmatic peritoneum: Peritoneal strips in aggregate 11 x 6.5 cm with focal nodularity (deposits up to 0.5 cm). Representative sections in 3 cassettes.

Part J — Pelvic lymph nodes: Fibrofatty tissue submitted from right external iliac, left external iliac, right internal iliac, left internal iliac, right obturator, and left obturator nodal stations. In aggregate 22 lymph nodes identified, ranging from 0.3 to 2.1 cm in greatest dimension. All nodes submitted in 10 cassettes.

Part K — Para-aortic lymph nodes: Fibrofatty tissue from right and left infrarenal para-aortic regions. In aggregate 14 lymph nodes identified, ranging from 0.4 to 1.4 cm in greatest dimension. All nodes submitted in 6 cassettes.

---

MICROSCOPIC FINDINGS

Part A — Right fallopian tube and right ovary:
Fallopian tube: The fimbriated end demonstrates a discrete focus of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). The STIC lesion is characterized by stratified epithelial cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, loss of normal ciliated cell architecture, nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and increased mitotic activity. Immunohistochemical staining confirms: p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression pattern in STIC cells, consistent with TP53 missense mutation; wildtype background tubal epithelium shows heterogeneous weak staining), Ki-67 markedly elevated in STIC cells (~80%). The proximal tube is uninvolved. Serosal surface of tube involved by carcinoma.
Right ovary: Ovarian cortex and surface are diffusely replaced by high-grade serous carcinoma. Characteristic features include: complex papillary and micropapillary architecture, slit-like glandular spaces lined by markedly atypical cells with high nuclear grade (nuclear atypia, prominent nucleoli, macronucleoli), psammoma bodies present (moderate number), geographic areas of tumor necrosis, and a brisk mitotic rate exceeding 15 mitoses per 10 high-power fields. Lymphovascular invasion is identified.

Part B — Left fallopian tube and left ovary:
Fallopian tube: A second STIC lesion is identified in the fimbriated end of the left fallopian tube, morphologically identical to that in the right tube. p53 aberrant immunostaining confirmed. This bilateral STIC involvement strongly supports fallopian tube as the site of origin.
Left ovary: High-grade serous carcinoma involving ovarian cortex and surface, morphologically identical to the right ovary. Extensive surface involvement. No uninvolved cortical tissue identified in sampled sections.

Part C — Uterus and cervix:
Endometrium: Atrophic endometrium, no atypia, no carcinoma.
Myometrium: Three leiomyomata are confirmed microscopically (0.4 cm, 1.2 cm, and 1.8 cm), composed of bland interlacing smooth muscle bundles. No malignant transformation. No carcinoma in the myometrium.
Cervix: Unremarkable squamous and endocervical mucosa. No carcinoma. No high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion.
Parametria: No carcinoma.

Part D — Infracolic omentum:
The omentum is extensively replaced by high-grade serous carcinoma with a desmoplastic stromal response. Tumor is arranged in nests, papillary clusters, and solid sheets. Extensive tumor necrosis present. Psammoma bodies identified throughout. Lymphovascular spaces within omental fat contain tumor emboli. Surgical excision margins are negative for carcinoma.

Part E — Appendix:
The appendix shows no evidence of carcinoma. Mild periappendiceal adipose tissue fibrosis present. No appendiceal mucinous neoplasm. Serosal surface is uninvolved.

Part F — Right pelvic peritoneum:
Multiple foci of high-grade serous carcinoma are present on the peritoneal surface and within subperitoneal fibrous tissue. Tumor nests with papillary architecture, psammoma bodies, and high-grade nuclear features. Surgical resection margins are negative.

Part G — Left pelvic peritoneum:
High-grade serous carcinoma involving peritoneal surface, morphologically similar to right side. Margins negative.

Part H — Right diaphragmatic peritoneum:
Seven discrete foci of high-grade serous carcinoma are identified on the diaphragmatic peritoneal surface, ranging from 0.1 to 0.9 cm. The largest deposit shows central necrosis. All deposits appear completely excised within the submitted specimen. No involvement of underlying diaphragmatic muscle fibers.

Part I — Left diaphragmatic peritoneum:
Three foci of high-grade serous carcinoma on peritoneal surface, up to 0.5 cm. Completely excised.

Part J — Pelvic lymph nodes:
Metastatic high-grade serous carcinoma identified in 4 of 22 pelvic lymph nodes:
  - Right external iliac: 1/4 positive (largest deposit 1.3 cm, with extranodal extension into perinodal fibroadipose tissue)
  - Left external iliac: 1/4 positive (0.6 cm deposit)
  - Right obturator: 1/4 positive (0.4 cm deposit)
  - Left internal iliac: 1/5 positive (0.5 cm deposit)
  - Right internal iliac: 0/3 negative
  - Left obturator: 0/2 negative
The 18 remaining lymph nodes show no evidence of carcinoma (sinus histiocytosis noted in several reactive nodes).

Part K — Para-aortic lymph nodes:
Metastatic high-grade serous carcinoma in 2 of 14 para-aortic lymph nodes:
  - Right para-aortic: 1/7 positive (0.8 cm, no extranodal extension)
  - Left para-aortic: 1/7 positive (0.5 cm)
The 12 remaining nodes are negative.

Part L — Liver surface implant:
A single 0.6 cm focus of high-grade serous carcinoma involving the liver capsule (Glisson's capsule) with superficial fibrous tissue involvement. No invasion of hepatic parenchyma. This is consistent with a peritoneal implant on the liver surface (Glisson's capsule deposit), not a parenchymal metastasis. Surgical excision complete.

---

ANCILLARY STUDIES

Immunohistochemistry (performed on representative sections from Part A, confirmed on Part D):
- WT1: Positive (diffuse, strong nuclear staining in tumor cells)
- PAX8: Positive (nuclear, diffuse)
- CK7: Positive (diffuse, strong)
- CK20: Negative
- Estrogen receptor (ER): Positive (approximately 25-30% of tumor nuclei, moderate intensity)
- Progesterone receptor (PR): Negative
- p53: Aberrant pattern — diffuse strong overexpression in the vast majority of tumor cells (>95%), consistent with TP53 gain-of-function missense mutation (as opposed to null pattern); normal heterogeneous staining in benign stromal cells serves as internal positive control
- Ki-67: Approximately 70-75% proliferative index in high-grade tumor areas
- p16: Diffuse strong block positivity (non-specific in HGSC)
- BRCA1 protein (by IHC): Loss of nuclear expression in tumor cells (staining retained in tumor-associated stromal cells, serving as internal control) — consistent with biallelic BRCA1 inactivation
- BerEP4: Positive
- Calretinin: Negative (excludes mesothelioma)
- MOC31: Positive

Note: The IHC profile (WT1+, PAX8+, CK7+, CK20-, ER+, p53 aberrant) is characteristic of high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin.

Note on STIC confirmation: Both right and left fallopian tube STIC lesions show the p53-signature pattern with aberrant overexpression, identical to the invasive carcinoma component, supporting clonal origin from the tubal epithelium.

Molecular/Ancillary note: Somatic tumor tissue submitted to molecular pathology for next-generation sequencing per institutional protocol for gynecologic malignancies (results reported separately under molecular pathology accession).

DIAGNOSIS

Part A — RIGHT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND RIGHT OVARY:
1. Right fallopian tube: SEROUS TUBAL INTRAEPITHELIAL CARCINOMA (STIC), fimbriated end; p53 aberrant; serosal surface involved by carcinoma.
2. Right ovary: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 8.1 cm, with involvement of ovarian cortex and surface; lymphovascular invasion present.

Part B — LEFT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND LEFT OVARY:

2. Left ovary: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 5.9 cm, with extensive surface involvement.

Part C — UTERUS AND CERVIX:
- Atrophic endometrium; no carcinoma.
- Uterine leiomyomata, multiple (up to 1.8 cm).
- Cervix: no carcinoma, no dysplasia.
- Parametria: no carcinoma.

Part D — INFRACOLIC OMENTUM:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, extensive involvement (omental cake 11.5 x 7.8 cm); margins negative.

Part E — APPENDIX:
- No carcinoma. No appendiceal neoplasm.

Part F — RIGHT PELVIC PERITONEUM:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, multiple foci; margins negative.

Part G — LEFT PELVIC PERITONEUM:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA; margins negative.

Part H — RIGHT DIAPHRAGMATIC PERITONEUM:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 7 foci (up to 0.9 cm); completely excised.

Part I — LEFT DIAPHRAGMATIC PERITONEUM:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 3 foci (up to 0.5 cm); completely excised.

Part J — PELVIC LYMPH NODES (22 nodes examined):
- METASTATIC HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 4/22 lymph nodes positive; largest metastatic deposit 1.3 cm; extranodal extension present in one node (right external iliac station).

Part K — PARA-AORTIC LYMPH NODES (14 nodes examined):
- METASTATIC HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, 2/14 lymph nodes positive; largest deposit 0.8 cm; no extranodal extension.

Part L — LIVER SURFACE (GLISSON'S CAPSULE) IMPLANT:
- HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, single focus, 0.6 cm, involving hepatic capsule only; no parenchymal invasion; completely excised.

---

PATHOLOGIC STAGING (AJCC 8th Edition / FIGO 2014)

Primary Tumor (pT): pT3c — Peritoneal metastases beyond pelvis, >2 cm in greatest dimension, including involvement of omentum, diaphragmatic peritoneum, and liver surface capsule (Glisson's capsule implant WITHOUT parenchymal involvement remains pT3c/FIGO IIIC)
Regional Lymph Nodes (pN): pN1 — Retroperitoneal lymph node metastases (pelvic and para-aortic nodes involved; pelvic node largest deposit 1.3 cm)
Distant Metastasis (pM): Not applicable (liver surface/capsular implant classified as peritoneal disease, not parenchymal; pM0 by AJCC)

FINAL PATHOLOGIC STAGE: pT3c pN1 M0 — FIGO Stage IIIC

Cytoreduction Status: R0 — No macroscopic residual disease. All submitted specimens with negative margins. No gross residual carcinoma identified at completion of surgery per operative and pathologic assessment.

Comment: Bilateral STIC lesions with identical p53 aberrant immunophenotype to invasive carcinoma strongly support origin from fallopian tube epithelium. The overall morphologic, immunohistochemical, and molecular profile is fully consistent with HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA of fallopian tube/ovarian origin. The liver surface deposit represents a capsular/peritoneal implant without hepatic parenchymal invasion, and is therefore classified as peritoneal disease (consistent with pT3c/FIGO IIIC, not FIGO Stage IV). Lymphovascular invasion is identified and extranodal extension is present in the right external iliac nodal station.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Four-week post-operative visit following primary debulking surgery for stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. Patient presenting for wound check, performance status assessment, and initiation planning for adjuvant chemotherapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with a recently confirmed diagnosis of FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary/fallopian tube, harboring a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation). She initially presented several months ago with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, 12-lb unintentional weight loss, and pelvic pressure. Staging work-up revealed bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm), large-volume ascites (~2.5 L), extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking, diaphragmatic implants, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. CA-125 at diagnosis was 1,847 U/mL. Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma (WT1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant, ER 30%), and germline/somatic NGS confirmed BRCA1 biallelic inactivation with HRD-positive tumor (HRD score 56), TP53 p.R248W, and low TMB.

She subsequently underwent primary debulking surgery approximately 4 weeks ago: total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral diaphragmatic peritoneal stripping, pelvic peritoneal resection, and excision of paracolic gutter implants and a small liver surface implant. Surgical pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma arising from the fallopian tube fimbriae (bilateral STIC lesions), with involvement of bilateral ovaries, omentum, pelvic and diaphragmatic peritoneum, liver surface (single 0.6 cm focus), and lymph nodes (4/22 pelvic nodes positive, 2/14 para-aortic nodes positive). Cytoreduction was complete - R0 with no macroscopic residual disease. Estimated blood loss was 650 mL; she received 1 unit of pRBCs intraoperatively.

Post-operatively, her course was unremarkable. Bowel function returned on POD 3; she was discharged on POD 5 without complications. She presents today for her scheduled 4-week post-operative visit. She reports overall doing well at home. Her energy has slowly been improving. She is eating full meals without difficulty. She has noted mild residual abdominal soreness around her incision, but this is improving daily. She denies fever, chills, wound drainage, new abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. She has resumed light activity around the house. She is anxious about starting chemotherapy but engaged and motivated.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the fallopian tube/ovary, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), HRD-positive tumor, now 4 weeks post primary debulking surgery with R0 resection. She is recovering well and is now ready to begin adjuvant chemotherapy.

1. OVARIAN CANCER - STAGE IIIC HGSC, POST-R0 DEBULKING, BRCA1 GERMLINE:
   - Excellent post-operative recovery. R0 resection achieved - this is the single most important prognostic factor for long-term outcome in advanced ovarian cancer.
   - CA-125 declining appropriately (1,847 --> 342 U/mL at day 21 post-op). Trajectory expected to continue downward with adjuvant chemotherapy.
   - Will proceed with adjuvant platinum/taxane chemotherapy as discussed at initial consultation.
   - Regimen: Paraplatin AUC 6 / Taxol 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days x 6 cycles.
     -- carbo dose by Calvert formula: Target AUC 6 x (GFR 84 + 25) = approximately 654 mg; rounded to standard preparation.
     -- Taxol: BSA 1.80 m2 x 175 mg/m2 = 315 mg.
   - Pre-medications: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV (standard hypersensitivity prophylaxis).
   - Anti-emetic prophylaxis: ondansetron + dexamethasone; lorazepam and prochlorperazine as breakthrough.
   - Patient counseled on expected side effects: alopecia (nearly universal with PTX), nausea/vomiting, fatigue, myelosuppression (particularly neutropenia and thrombocytopenia from Paraplatin), and cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy from pacli. Patient educated to report fever ≥100.4°F (potential neutropenic fever), bleeding/bruising, or worsening numbness/tingling.
   - Cycle 1 scheduled for approximately 1 week from today (aiming for 5-6 weeks post-operatively to allow full post-surgical recovery).
   - CBC to be checked prior to each cycle; LFTs and CMP prior to each cycle.

2. GERMLINE BRCA1 PATHOGENIC VARIANT (c.5266dupC, 5382insC):
   - Germline testing confirmed at diagnostic laparoscopy phase; genetic counseling previously completed.
   - Germline BRCA1 status will be key for maintenance therapy planning after completion of platinum/taxane chemotherapy: patient is an ideal candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance given BRCA1 germline mutation, HRD-positive tumor, and expected complete response to platinum. Will revisit Lynparza vs. nira options at end of chemotherapy.
   - Cascade testing: daughter (age 24) referred for BRCA1 genetic counseling per prior recommendation.
   - BRCA1-related breast cancer risk: post-BSO (ovarian risk addressed surgically). Annual mammography and breast MRI recommended - will coordinate with breast surgery/high-risk program.

3. POST-OPERATIVE ANEMIA:
   - Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL - likely a combination of surgical blood loss (received 1 unit pRBCs intraoperatively), bone marrow suppression from forthcoming chemotherapy anticipated. Will monitor CBC closely.
   - No transfusion required at this time. Iron studies and B12/folate to be checked if anemia worsens or does not improve.
   - ESA use deferred for now; will reassess during chemotherapy.

4. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE/VASOMOTOR SYMPTOMS:
   - Patient experienced natural menopause at age 50; BSO at time of surgery does not represent a new hormonal change clinically, but she has noted some worsening of vasomotor symptoms post-operatively.
   - Hormone replacement therapy is contraindicated given BRCA1 carrier status and ovarian cancer diagnosis (ER+ primary).
   - Non-hormonal management options discussed: venlafaxine (SNRIs are evidence-based for hot flashes), gabapentin. Will discuss further at next visit if symptoms persist or worsen.
   - Vaginal dryness: topical vaginal moisturizers and lubricants (non-hormonal) recommended.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE:
   - Patient expresses appropriate anxiety about diagnosis and treatment. Encouraged open communication. Oncology nurse navigator will follow throughout treatment course.
   - Ovarian cancer support group information provided.
   - Social work referral offered; patient declined at this time but will reconsider.

Follow-up: Return to clinic in approximately 3 weeks (at time of Cycle 2) for toxicity assessment, CBC review, and clinical examination. CA-125 to be repeated at Cycle 3 mid-treatment assessment and end of treatment.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes. Visit included review of pathology and NGS results, risk-benefit discussion of adjuvant chemotherapy, discussion of BRCA1 implications, and patient education.
